title: Binary search for the median cut
summary: Search cut positions in the shorter array until the two sorted arrays form one balanced, globally ordered partition.
learning_objective: See the median as a rank boundary: one cut determines the other, and failed cross-boundary comparisons reveal which half of the cut search is impossible.
invariant: Every candidate keeps exactly six values in the combined left half; binary search changes only which values occupy that half.

phases.title: Turn median into a cut
phases.summary: Fix the left-half size and reduce two cuts to one independent choice.

phases.title: Eliminate bad cuts
phases.summary: Use both failure directions to discard impossible cut positions.

phases.title: Certify the partition
phases.summary: Check the only two cross-array relationships that sorted order does not guarantee.

phases.title: Read the median
phases.summary: Recover the middle rank directly from the valid partition boundaries.

valid_partition.start: if left1 <= right2 and left2 <= right1:

steps.title: Do not merge the arrays
steps.description: The combined collection contains 11 values, so its median is the value at rank 6. We only need to identify the boundary around that rank.
steps.insight: Merging would reveal every rank but cost linear time. The sorted inputs let us search directly for the one boundary that matters.

steps.title: Search the shorter array
steps.description: A has 5 values and B has 6, so A is already the shorter array. Its six gaps are the complete binary-search domain.
steps.insight: Searching the shorter array gives O(log(min(m, n))) time and keeps the complementary cut inside the longer array.

steps.title: Fix six places on the left
steps.description: left_size = (11 + 1) // 2 = 6. If cut A takes i values, cut B must take exactly 6 - i.
steps.insight: The size equation removes one degree of freedom. Binary search chooses only cut A; cut B is forced.

steps.title: Try the middle cut
steps.description: The midpoint of [0, 5] is cut A = 2. The size invariant immediately forces cut B = 6 - 2 = 4.
steps.insight: No matter whether this candidate succeeds, the combined left side contains exactly 2 + 4 = 6 values.

steps.title: Inspect only four boundary values
steps.description: The cuts expose A.left = 3, A.right = 8, B.left = 9, and B.right = 10. Internal values cannot create a new violation because each array is sorted.
steps.insight: Four values summarize the global ordering question; every other value is dominated by one of these boundaries.

steps.title: B contributes a value that is too large
steps.description: 3 ≤ 10 passes, but 9 ≤ 8 fails. B.left belongs on the left yet exceeds A.right on the right.
steps.insight: Predict the repair: A must contribute more values to the left, which simultaneously makes B contribute fewer.

steps.title: Discard cuts 0, 1, and 2
steps.description: Set low = cut A + 1 = 3. Moving A farther left would only make A.right smaller and B.left larger, so those cuts cannot work.
steps.insight: The direction is justified by monotonic boundary motion, not by trial and error. One comparison eliminates half the remaining candidates.

steps.title: Try the new midpoint
steps.description: The midpoint of [3, 5] is cut A = 4, forcing cut B = 2. The left half still contains exactly six values.
steps.insight: The candidate jumps across the unknown valid cut, which lets this example expose the opposite failure direction next.

steps.title: Read the new boundary quartet
steps.description: The boundaries are now A.left = 12, A.right = 15, B.left = 4, and B.right = 6.
steps.insight: Moving A right raised its left boundary from 3 to 12 while moving B left lowered its right boundary from 10 to 6.

steps.title: A now contributes a value that is too large
steps.description: 12 ≤ 6 fails while 4 ≤ 15 passes. A.left cannot remain in the lower half when B.right is smaller.
steps.insight: This is the mirror image of the first failure. A's cut has crossed too far to the right.

steps.title: Discard cuts 4 and 5
steps.description: Set high = cut A - 1 = 3. Larger cuts only raise A.left and lower B.right, so neither can repair 12 > 6.
steps.insight: Both binary-search directions have now been derived from the same pair of cross-boundary inequalities.

steps.title: Only cut 3 remains
steps.description: With low = high = 3, choose cut A = 3 and force cut B = 3. The left side again contains exactly six values.
steps.insight: Binary search has isolated the only possible cut without comparing or merging the arrays element by element.

steps.title: Expose the decisive four values
steps.description: The final candidate gives A.left = 8, A.right = 12, B.left = 6, and B.right = 9.
steps.insight: Within-array order is automatic. Only 8 versus 9 and 6 versus 12 remain to certify global order.

steps.title: Both cross checks pass
steps.description: 8 ≤ 9 and 6 ≤ 12. Together with sorted order inside A and B, these checks prove that every left-side value is at most every right-side value.
steps.insight: The size invariant proves the correct rank; the two inequalities prove the correct ordering. Those are exactly the two ingredients needed for the median.

steps.title: Read rank 6 from the left boundary
steps.description: Because the total length is odd, the left half owns the extra value. Its maximum is max(8, 6) = 8, which is the median.
steps.insight: Only three of six possible cuts were examined. That shrinking cut search gives logarithmic time while the visualization stores only a few indices and boundary values.
